title: lsr Maintenance Plan
text: Goal: Make documentation accurate, consistent, beginner-friendly across 29 exported functions print methods. Guiding principle — reader novice: audience introductory statistics students encountering R first time. Documentation must reflect : Avoid S3/OOP jargon. “S3 method”, “dispatch”, “generic function”, “class attribute” mean nothing beginner. Rewrite plain English. Describe user sees, R internally. functions whose primary purpose printing (t-test functions, (), correlate(), etc.), @return describe printed output — lead “returns object class X”. @examples self-contained, immediately runnable, use consistent code style (spaces around =, spaces commas, extra spaces ( )). Fixing content code style existing examples explicitly within scope. Specific checks per file: - @description — one clear sentence; remove redundant hedges like “convenience function intended pedagogical purposes ”. - @param — parameters documented; plain language (“logical flag”, “set TRUE …”). - @return — highest priority revision; describe user sees. - @details — verify accuracy Stages 1–3 changes. - @seealso — cross-references relevant complete. - @examples — self-contained, runnable, consistent code style; add examples missing; improve existing examples style clarity falls short. - Print methods — minimal plain-English documentation; note called automatically object printed, without using word “dispatch”. Approach: One PR per function group. Run devtools::document() batch verify .Rd files regenerate cleanly.

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: Throughout article use data imaginary study. Thirty students three tutorial groups (, B, C) sat end--term exam. exam attended one-hour study-skills workshop; score short practice test (score_pre) (score_post) workshop. dataset also records many hours per week student typically studies whether passed exam (score 65 counts pass). first rows:
code: set.seed(250)  n <- 30 students <- data.frame(   id          = 1:n,   group       = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 10)),   gender      = factor(sample(c("female", "male"), n, replace = TRUE)),   score       = pmax(40, pmin(100, c(     round(rnorm(10, mean = 62, sd = 7)),  # group A     round(rnorm(10, mean = 68, sd = 7)),  # group B     round(rnorm(10, mean = 74, sd = 7))   # group C   ))),   study_hours = NA_real_,   score_pre   = NA_integer_,   score_post  = NA_integer_ ) students$study_hours <- round(students$score / 10 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.8), 1) students$score_pre   <- pmax(30L, round(students$score - rnorm(n, mean = 8, sd = 3))) students$score_post  <- students$score students$passed      <- factor(ifelse(students$score >= 65, "yes", "no")) head(students) #>   id group gender score study_hours score_pre score_post passed #> 1  1     A   male    68         6.6        58         68    yes #> 2  2     A female    54         5.5        44         54     no #> 3  3     A   male    58         5.2        44         58     no #> 4  4     A   male    62         6.5        50         62     no #> 5  5     A   male    64         7.4        60         64     no #> 6  6     A   male    66         6.6        59         66    yes

code: modeOf(students$passed) #> [1] "yes"

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: correlate() produces correlation matrix numeric columns data frame. can also pass single data frame select numeric columns automatically, supply subset columns: Students study tend score higher — correlation 0.64. diagonal left blank variable’s correlation always exactly 1, carries information.
code: correlate(students[, c("score", "study_hours")]) #>  #> CORRELATIONS #> ============ #> - correlation type:  pearson  #> - correlations shown only when both variables are numeric #>  #>             score study_hours #> score           .       0.779 #> study_hours 0.779           .

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: Many statistical functions R expect data long format (one observation per row), data sometimes collected stored wide format (one participant per row, repeated measurements separate columns). wideToLong() longToWide() functions convert two. students data frame two score columns — score_pre score_post — follow naming convention measurement_condition. wideToLong() uses underscore separator split column name measurement variable (score) within-subject condition variable (time): long format one row per student per time point instead one row per student. go way, longToWide() takes long-format data formula telling column spread across conditions: two data frames contain information, just arranged differently.
code: scores_wide <- students[, c("id", "group", "score_pre", "score_post")] scores_long <- wideToLong(data = scores_wide, within = "time", sep = "_") head(scores_long) #>   id group time score #> 1  1     A  pre    58 #> 2  2     A  pre    44 #> 3  3     A  pre    44 #> 4  4     A  pre    50 #> 5  5     A  pre    60 #> 6  6     A  pre    59 scores_back <- longToWide(data = scores_long, formula = score ~ time, sep = "_") head(scores_back) #>   id group score_pre score_post #> 1  1     A        58         68 #> 2  2     A        44         54 #> 3  3     A        44         58 #> 4  4     A        50         62 #> 5  5     A        60         64 #> 6  6     A        59         66

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: lsr provides wrapper functions six common hypothesis tests. one prints structured summary states hypotheses, gives descriptive statistics, reports test result alongside effect size.

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: one-sample t-test asks whether population mean equal specified value. test whether true mean exam score differs 65 (pass mark): mean score (67.8) 65, p-value 0.060 conventional 0.05 threshold. borderline result — evidence suggests scores may pass mark average, sample 30 students small enough confident. Notice output includes Cohen’s d (0.36), tells us effect size small moderate.
code: oneSampleTTest(x = students$score, mu = 65) #>  #>    One sample t-test  #>  #> Data variable:   students$score  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>              score #>    mean     68.167 #>    std dev.  9.699 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population mean equals 65  #>    alternative: population mean not equal to 65  #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  1.788  #>    degrees of freedom:  29  #>    p-value:  0.084  #>  #> Other information:  #>    two-sided 95% confidence interval:  [64.545, 71.788]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  0.326

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: independent-samples t-test compares means two separate groups. compare groups B see different average scores: mean score group B (68.0) 6.9 points higher group (61.1), difference clearly significant (p = 0.002). Cohen’s d 1.62 indicates large effect — groups differ considerably one pooled standard deviation.
code: ab <- droplevels(students[students$group %in% c("A", "B"), ]) independentSamplesTTest(formula = score ~ group, data = ab) #>  #>    Welch's independent samples t-test  #>  #> Outcome variable:   score  #> Grouping variable:  group  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>                  A      B #>    mean     60.800 68.900 #>    std dev.  6.143  6.887 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population means equal for both groups #>    alternative: different population means in each group #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  -2.776  #>    degrees of freedom:  17.769  #>    p-value:  0.013  #>  #> Other information:  #>    two-sided 95% confidence interval:  [-14.237, -1.963]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  1.241

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: paired t-test compares two measurements participants. use long-format scores_long data created earlier test whether scores improved study workshop: Scores improved average 8.2 points workshop, difference highly significant (p < 0.001). large Cohen’s d 3.19 reflects improvement consistent across students — standard deviation difference (2.6 points) much smaller mean change.
code: scores_long$id <- factor(scores_long$id) pairedSamplesTTest(formula = score ~ time, data = scores_long, id = "id") #>  #>    Paired samples t-test  #>  #> Outcome variable:   score  #> Grouping variable:  time  #> ID variable:        id  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>               post    pre difference #>    mean     60.100 68.167     -8.067 #>    std dev. 10.993  9.699      3.151 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population means equal for both measurements #>    alternative: different population means for each measurement #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  -14.024  #>    degrees of freedom:  29  #>    p-value:  <.001  #>  #> Other information:  #>    two-sided 95% confidence interval:  [-9.243, -6.89]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  2.56

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: associationTest() tests whether two categorical variables independent one another. ask whether passing rates differ female male students: output shows observed expected contingency tables, making easy see data diverge null hypothesis. Male students passed higher rate (81%) female students (43%), 30 students p-value 0.072, strong enough evidence conclude real association. good example result pattern looks interesting sample small sure.
code: associationTest(formula = ~ gender + passed, data = students) #> Warning in associationTest(formula = ~gender + passed, data = students): #> Expected frequencies too small: chi-squared approximation may be incorrect #>  #>      Chi-square test of categorical association #>  #> Variables:   gender, passed  #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        variables are independent of one another #>    alternative: some contingency exists between variables #>  #> Observed contingency table: #>         passed #> gender   no yes #>   female  4   9 #>   male    7  10 #>  #> Expected contingency table under the null hypothesis: #>         passed #> gender     no   yes #>   female 4.77  8.23 #>   male   6.23 10.77 #>  #> Test results:  #>    X-squared statistic:  0.042  #>    degrees of freedom:  1  #>    p-value:  0.838  #>  #> Other information:  #>    estimated effect size (Cramer's v):  0.037  #>    Yates' continuity correction has been applied #>    warning: expected frequencies too small, results may be inaccurate

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: goodness--fit test asks whether observed frequencies categorical variable match set expected proportions. check whether students distributed equally across three tutorial groups: design, three groups perfectly balanced (10 students ), p = 1. real study use test theoretical expectation distribution — example, testing whether proportion students achieving grade matches historical benchmark.
code: goodnessOfFitTest(x = students$group) #>  #>      Chi-square test against specified probabilities #>  #> Data variable:   students$group  #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        true probabilities are as specified #>    alternative: true probabilities differ from those specified #>  #> Descriptives:  #>   observed freq. expected freq. specified prob. #> A             10             10       0.3333333 #> B             10             10       0.3333333 #> C             10             10       0.3333333 #>  #> Test results:  #>    X-squared statistic:  0  #>    degrees of freedom:  2  #>    p-value:  1

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: ANOVA finds overall difference across three groups, often want know pairs groups differ. posthocPairwiseT() runs pairwise t-tests correction multiple comparisons. takes result aov() input: three pairs groups differ significantly Holm correction. smallest p-value versus C comparison, unsurprising given group mean 61 group C mean 74.
code: score_model <- aov(score ~ group, data = students) posthocPairwiseT(score_model) #>  #>  Pairwise comparisons using t tests with pooled SD  #>  #> data:  score and group  #>  #>   A      B      #> B 0.0647 -      #> C 0.0017 0.1119 #>  #> P value adjustment method: holm

title: A guided overview of lsr

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: Cohen’s d measures size difference two groups standard deviation units. already saw appear automatically t-test output, can also compute directly: value 1.62 large effect conventional benchmarks (small ≈ 0.2, medium ≈ 0.5, large ≈ 0.8).
code: cohensD(formula = score ~ group, data = ab) #> [1] 1.241267

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: Eta-squared measures proportion variance outcome explained categorical predictor. ANOVA equivalent R-squared regression. Pass aov() result: Eta-squared 0.495 means tutorial group explains 49% variance exam scores. large effect — group student belongs strongly predictive score.
code: etaSquared(score_model) #>          eta.sq eta.sq.part #> group 0.3621724   0.3621724

title: A guided overview of lsr
text: Cramér’s V effect size chi-square test association. runs 0 (association) 1 (perfect association). Pass contingency table: value 0.33 suggests moderate association gender passing status, consistent pattern saw contingency table earlier — even though chi-square test reach significance small sample.
code: cramersV(table(students$gender, students$passed)) #> Warning in stats::chisq.test(...): Chi-squared approximation may be incorrect #> [1] 0.0372238

title: Grouped bar plots with error bars — bars
text: formula two-sided formula form response ~ group1 response ~ group1 + group2. response variable must numeric; grouping variables must factors. data optional data frame containing variables named formula. omitted, variables looked workspace. heightFun function used calculate bar heights. Defaults mean. Must return single number. errorFun function used calculate error bar positions. Defaults ciMean. Must return two numbers (lower upper bounds). Set FALSE suppress error bars. yLabel y-axis label. Defaults name response variable. xLabels Labels x-axis tick marks. Defaults levels group1. main plot title. Defaults title. ylim numeric vector length 2 giving y-axis limits. lower bound defaults 0; upper bound estimated automatically. barFillColour vector colours used fill bars. Defaults pastel rainbow palette. barLineWidth width bar border lines. Defaults 2. barLineColour colour bar border lines. Defaults "black". barSpaceSmall gap bars within cluster, proportion bar width. Defaults 0.2. barSpaceBig gap separating clusters bars, proportion bar width. Defaults 1. legendLabels Labels legend entries. Defaults levels group2. Set FALSE suppress legend. legend drawn one grouping variable specified. legendDownShift far top plot place legend, proportion plot height. Defaults 0. legendLeftShift far right edge place legend, proportion plot width. Defaults 0. errorBarLineWidth line width error bars. Defaults 1. errorBarLineColour colour error bars. Defaults "grey40". errorBarWhiskerWidth width error bar whiskers, proportion bar width. Defaults 0.2.

Missing values removed warning. least 2 complete cases required per group.

title: Grouped bar plots with error bars — bars
code: # one grouping factor df <- data.frame(   outcome = c(3, 4, 5, 2, 4, 6, 5, 7, 8),   group = factor(c("a", "a", "a", "b", "b", "b", "c", "c", "c")) ) bars(outcome ~ group, data = df) #> Warning: longer object length is not a multiple of shorter object length #> Warning: longer object length is not a multiple of shorter object length   # two grouping factors df2 <- data.frame(   outcome = c(3, 4, 5, 2, 4, 6, 5, 7, 8, 4, 3, 6),   group1  = factor(rep(c("a", "b"), each = 6)),   group2  = factor(rep(c("x", "y", "z"), times = 4)) ) bars(outcome ~ group1 + group2, data = df2) #> Warning: data have zero variance

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
text: Calculates Cohen's d measure effect size.

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
code: cohensD(   x = NULL,   y = NULL,   data = NULL,   method = "pooled",   mu = 0,   formula = NULL )

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
text: x numeric vector data group 1, formula form outcome ~ group (case data can used supply data frame). y numeric vector data group 2. Omit one-sample calculation. data optional data frame containing variables x x formula. method version Cohen's d calculate. Options "pooled" (default), "x.sd", "y.sd", "corrected", "raw", "paired", "unequal". See Details. mu null value one-sample calculation. Almost always 0 (default). formula formula form outcome ~ group. alternative way supply formula instead using x.

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
text: single positive number: magnitude effect size d. sign mean difference dropped, value always zero greater.

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
text: function can used two main ways. two separate numeric vectors, call cohensD(x = group1, y = group2). data data frame grouping variable, use formula: cohensD(outcome ~ group, data = mydata). method argument controls standard deviation estimated: "pooled" Pooled SD groups (matches Student's     t-test). default. "corrected" Bias-corrected version "pooled",     multiplied (N-3)/(N-2.25). "raw" Like "pooled" divides N rather     N-2. "x.sd" SD first group . "y.sd" SD second group . "unequal" Square root average two group     variances (matches Welch's t-test). "paired" SD within-person differences (matches     paired-samples t-test). one-sample calculation, supply x (optionally mu). result abs(mean(x) - mu) / sd(x).

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical power analysis behavioral sciences (2nd ed.). Hillsdale, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.

title: Cohen's d — cohensD
code: # two independent groups supplied as separate vectors gradesA <- c(55, 65, 65, 68, 70) # 5 students with teacher A gradesB <- c(56, 60, 62, 66)     # 4 students with teacher B cohensD(gradesA, gradesB) #> [1] 0.699892  # the same comparison using a formula and a data frame grade <- c(55, 65, 65, 68, 70, 56, 60, 62, 66) teacher <- c("A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "B", "B", "B", "B") cohensD(grade ~ teacher) #> [1] 0.699892  # paired samples: use method = "paired" (SD of within-person differences) pre  <- c(100, 122, 97, 25, 274) post <- c(104, 125, 99, 29, 277) cohensD(pre, post, method = "paired") #> [1] 3.824732  # equivalent one-sample calculation on the difference scores cohensD(post - pre) #> [1] 3.824732  # formula interface with a data frame exams <- data.frame(grade, teacher) cohensD(grade ~ teacher, data = exams) #> [1] 0.699892

Defaults FALSE. corr.method type correlation compute: "pearson" (default), "spearman", "kendall". p.adjust.method method used correct p-values multiple comparisons. title: Correlation matrices — correlate
text: Prints correlation matrix. test = TRUE, also prints matrix adjusted p-values matrix sample sizes. results also returned list four elements: correlation (correlation matrix), p.value (matrix p-values), sample.size (matrix sample sizes), args (record options used). list can assigned variable inspected needed.

title: Correlation matrices — correlate
text: Calculates correlation matrix pairs numeric variables. x supplied, pairwise correlations among variables x computed. x y supplied, variables x correlated variables y. Non-numeric variables (e.g., factors) silently ignored: appear output NA place correlation values. makes convenient pass entire data frame without first removing categorical columns. test = TRUE, hypothesis tests run every pair numeric variables. reduce risk false positives testing many pairs , p-values adjusted using Holm method default. See p.adjust available methods. Missing data handled using pairwise complete cases, sample sizes may differ across pairs variables.

title: Correlation matrices — correlate
code: # data frame with factors and missing values data <- data.frame(   anxiety    = c(1.31, 2.72, 3.18, 4.21, 5.55, NA),   stress     = c(2.01, 3.45, 1.99, 3.25, 4.27, 6.80),   depression = c(2.51, 1.77, 3.34, 5.83, 9.01, 7.74),   happiness  = c(4.02, 3.66, 5.23, 6.37, 7.83, 1.18),   gender = factor(c("male", "female", "female", "male", "female", "female")),   ssri   = factor(c("no", "no", "no", NA, "yes", "yes")) )  # Pearson correlation matrix (the default) correlate(data) #>  #> CORRELATIONS #> ============ #> - correlation type:  pearson  #> - correlations shown only when both variables are numeric #>  #>            anxiety stress depression happiness gender ssri #> anxiety          .  0.784      0.906     0.924      .    . #> stress       0.784      .      0.693    -0.453      .    . #> depression   0.906  0.693          .     0.247      .    . #> happiness    0.924 -0.453      0.247         .      .    . #> gender           .      .          .         .      .    . #> ssri             .      .          .         .      .    .  # Spearman correlations correlate(data, corr.method = "spearman") #>  #> CORRELATIONS #> ============ #> - correlation type:  spearman  #> - correlations shown only when both variables are numeric #>  #>            anxiety stress depression happiness gender ssri #> anxiety          .  0.500      0.900     0.900      .    . #> stress       0.500      .      0.543    -0.257      .    . #> depression   0.900  0.543          .     0.429      .    . #> happiness    0.900 -0.257      0.429         .      .    . #> gender           .      .          .         .      .    . #> ssri             .      .          .         .      .    .  # correlate two subsets of variables with each other nervous <- data[, c("anxiety", "stress")] happy   <- data[, c("happiness", "depression")] correlate(nervous, happy) #>  #> CORRELATIONS #> ============ #> - code: df <- data.frame(   rt = c(451, 562, 704, 324, 505, 600, 829),   cond = factor(x = c(1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2), labels = c("group1", "group2")) )  # Welch's t-test (the default, does not assume equal variances) independentSamplesTTest(rt ~ cond, df) #>  #>    Welch's independent samples t-test  #>  #> Outcome variable:   rt  #> Grouping variable:  cond  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>              group1  group2 #>    mean     572.333 564.500 #>    std dev. 126.816 210.239 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population means equal for both groups #>    alternative: different population means in each group #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  0.061  #>    degrees of freedom:  4.89  #>    p-value:  0.954  #>  #> Other information:  #>    two-sided 95% confidence interval:  [-323.703, 339.37]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  0.045  #>   # Student's t-test (assumes equal variances) independentSamplesTTest(rt ~ cond, df, var.equal = TRUE) #>  #>    Student's independent samples t-test  #>  #> Outcome variable:   rt  #> Grouping variable:  cond  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>              group1  group2 #>    mean     572.333 564.500 #>    std dev. 126.816 210.239 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population means equal for both groups #>    alternative: different population means in each group #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  0.056  #>    degrees of freedom:  5  #>    p-value:  0.957  #>  #> Other information:  #>    two-sided 95% confidence interval:  [-348.567, 364.234]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  0.043  #>   # one-sided test: is group1 larger? independentSamplesTTest(rt ~ cond, df, one.sided = "group1") #>  #>    Welch's independent samples t-test  #>  #> Outcome variable:   rt  #> Grouping variable:  cond  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>              group1  group2 #>    mean     572.333 564.500 #>    std dev. 126.816 210.239 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population means are equal, or smaller for group 'group1'  #>    alternative: population mean is larger for group 'group1'  #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  0.061  #>    degrees of freedom:  4.89  #>    p-value:  0.477  #>  #> Other information:  #>    one-sided 95% confidence interval:  [-251.588, Inf]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  0.045  #>   # missing values are removed with a warning df$rt[1] <- NA df$cond[7] <- NA independentSamplesTTest(rt ~ cond, df) #> Warning: 2 case(s) removed due to missingness #>  #>    Welch's independent samples t-test  #>  #> Outcome variable:   rt  #> Grouping variable:  cond  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>              group1  group2 #>    mean     633.000 476.333 #>    std dev. 100.409 140.215 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #>    null:        population means equal for both groups #>    alternative: different population means in each group #>  #> Test results:  #>    t-statistic:  1.455  #>    degrees of freedom:  2.867  #>    p-value:  0.246  #>  #> Other information:  #>    two-sided 95% confidence interval:  [-195.166, 508.499]  #>    estimated effect size (Cohen's d):  1.285  #>

ID variable:        id  #>  #> Descriptive statistics:  #>             time1 time2 difference #>    mean     6.250 7.750     -1.500 #>    std dev. 2.500 3.500      1.291 #>  #> Hypotheses:  #> title: Standardised regression coefficients — standardCoefs
code: X1 <- c(0.69, 0.77, 0.92, 1.72, 1.79, 2.37, 2.64, 2.69, 2.84, 3.41) Y  <- c(3.28, 4.23, 3.34, 3.73, 5.33, 6.02, 5.16, 6.49, 6.49, 6.05)  # simple linear regression model1 <- lm(Y ~ X1) coefficients(model1)   # unstandardised #> (Intercept)          X1  #>    2.717160    1.156674  standardCoefs(model1)  # unstandardised and standardised side by side #>           b      beta #> X1 1.156674 0.8674478  # multiple regression X2 <- c(0.19, 0.22, 0.95, 0.43, 0.51, 0.04, 0.12, 0.44, 0.38, 0.33) model2 <- lm(Y ~ X1 + X2) standardCoefs(model2) #>             b        beta #> X1  1.1278652  0.84584304 #> X2 -0.4428046 -0.08903252  # model with an interaction term model3 <- lm(Y ~ X1 * X2) standardCoefs(model3) #>                b       beta #> X1     0.7945328  0.5958602 #> X2    -1.8409489 -0.3701504 #> X1:X2  1.0332433  0.3562668

Variables whose names contain sep treated repeated measures; others treated -subject variables. within character string, vector strings, giving name(s) use within-subject factor column(s) output. Defaults "within". sep separator string used wide-form variable names separate measure name factor level(s). Defaults "_". separator must appear anywhere else variable names. split Set TRUE (default) split multiple within-subject factors separate columns output. Set FALSE keep combined single column.

title: Reshape from wide to long — wideToLong

title: Reshape from wide to long — wideToLong
text: function companion longToWide. determines reshape structure variable names rather requiring explicit formula. naming scheme repeated-measures variables places measure name first, followed factor level(s), joined sep. example, variables named accuracy_t1 accuracy_t2 indicate measure called accuracy recorded two time points (t1 t2). reshaping, long-form output contains one column called accuracy factor column (named within argument) levels t1 t2. Designs multiple within-subject factors supported. example, MRT_cond1_day1 encodes measure MRT level cond1 one factor day1 another. Supply within = c("condition", "day") name output columns. Multiple measured variables per observation (e.g., MRT PC) also supported.
